questions.text: Which 2 strategies most effectively reduce the risk of missed ureteral injury during complex gynecologic laparoscopy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Routine use of prophylactic ureteral stents
B) Preoperative CT urogram in all endometriosis cases
C) Intraoperative cystoscopy with visualization of ureteral jets
D) Postoperative imaging only if hematuria develops
E) Careful dissection along the pelvic sidewall with identification of ureters

questions.explanation: Cystoscopy helps identify injuries immediately by confirming efflux from both ureteral orifices. Meticulous sidewall dissection with visual ureter identification reduces inadvertent injury.

questions.text: Which 3 findings should raise suspicion for retroperitoneal vascular injury during laparoscopy? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Pulsatile bleeding from the lateral pelvic sidewall
B) Expanding hematoma near the psoas muscle
C) Persistent venous oozing with normal visualization
D) Drop in blood pressure with no intraperitoneal bleeding
E) Loss of end-tidal CO₂ during port placement

questions.explanation: Arterial injury may appear as pulsatile bleeding; expanding retroperitoneal hematoma and hemodynamic instability without visible intraperitoneal bleeding are also concerning.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about secondary trocar placement are incorrect? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) Secondary ports should be placed under direct visualization
B) The inferior epigastric vessels lie medial to the lateral umbilical fold
C) Blunt trocars eliminate the risk of vessel injury
D) Transillumination helps identify safe port placement sites
E) Most injuries occur during primary trocar placement, not secondary

questions.explanation: Inferior epigastric vessels are lateral to the lateral umbilical fold. Blunt trocars lessen but do not eliminate vascular risk. Direct visualization and transillumination are good practices, and most injuries occur during primary entry.
questions.submodule: Module 10.3 – Injuries during Secondary Port Placement

questions.text: Which of the following are typical intraoperative clues suggesting small bowel injury? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: C) Bowel adherence to the abdominal wall
D) Persistent bubbling from small bowel on irrigation
E) Leakage of enteric contents during suction

questions.explanation: Thermal or mechanical injury may show serosal changes; bubbles or visible enteric contents indicate perforation. Mesenteric bleeding can accompany bowel trauma; adhesions alone are not diagnostic.

questions.text: Which 2 intraoperative tests are most reliable for assessing sigmoid colon integrity after suspected injury? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Serosal scratch test with gauze
B) Air leak test with saline immersion
D) Direct visual inspection with 30° scope

questions.explanation: Air leak (under saline) and dye enema are commonly used to detect occult defects. Inspection alone may miss subtle or thermal injuries; a gauze ‘scratch’ test is not validated.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most common sign of a delayed ureteral injury postoperatively?
questions.explanation: Fever (often with flank pain/ileus) is a common clue to occult ureteral injury.

questions.text: A transection injury is identified in the upper third of the ureter. What is the best next step?
questions.explanation: Upper/mid-ureter injuries are typically repaired with tension-free ureteroureterostomy over a stent; lower-third injuries favor ureteroneocystostomy ± psoas hitch.

questions.text: Which of the following increases the risk of delayed recognition of ureteral injury during laparoscopic hysterectomy?
questions.explanation: Routine cystoscopy increases intraoperative detection; omitting it raises the chance of delayed diagnosis.

questions.text: A retroperitoneal hematoma is noted during laparoscopy and appears stable and non-expanding. What is an appropriate next step in management?

answerOptions.text: C) Direct clamping of the hematoma

questions.explanation: Letting down pneumoperitoneum can unmask active bleeding and guide next steps.

questions.text: What is the initial management of an expanding retroperitoneal hematoma during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: D) Close and observe in recovery

questions.explanation: Immediate tamponade is key; prepare for potential conversion and vascular control.

questions.text: What is the mortality rate associated with delayed diagnosis of retroperitoneal vascular injury?
questions.explanation: Delayed recognition can be fatal—reported mortality up to ~33%.

questions.text: Which vessel is most commonly injured during right lower quadrant trocar placement?
questions.explanation: Runs along the posterior rectus sheath; vulnerable in lateral lower quadrant ports.
questions.submodule: Module 10.3 – Injuries during Secondary Port Placement

questions.text: What is an appropriate initial technique for controlling bleeding from an inferior epigastric vessel injury?

answerOptions.text: B) Placement of a urinary catheter balloon

questions.explanation: Foley balloon tamponade through the port tract provides rapid hemostasis.
questions.submodule: Module 10.3 – Injuries during Secondary Port Placement

questions.text: Which of the following is the least appropriate management step for inferior epigastric vessel injury?
questions.explanation: Most inferior epigastric injuries can be controlled laparoscopically; immediate laparotomy is seldom required.
questions.submodule: Module 10.3 – Injuries during Secondary Port Placement

questions.text: Which of the following is least appropriate when bowel injury is identified during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: B) Running the bowel to evaluate extent
C) Immediate laparotomy for all cases
E) Placing secondary ports for visualization

questions.explanation: Not all injuries require laparotomy; many are evaluated/repaired laparoscopically.

questions.text: What is the recommended method to close a 3 mm laceration of small bowel identified during laparoscopy?
questions.explanation: Transverse closure avoids luminal narrowing for small full-thickness lacerations.

questions.text: Which patient factor places someone at higher risk for entry-related small bowel injury?

answerOptions.text: D) History of Crohn’s disease and prior midline laparotomy

questions.explanation: Dense adhesions from IBD and prior midline laparotomy increase entry risk.

questions.text: What is the best technique to assess for small bowel injury intraoperatively?
questions.explanation: Systematic inspection (‘run the bowel’) is the gold standard intraoperatively.

questions.text: During the running of the bowel, what anatomical point should the surgeon begin with?
questions.explanation: Begin at the ileocecal junction and proceed proximally toward the ligament of Treitz.

questions.text: Which technique is most appropriate for evaluating the rectosigmoid colon for injury?
questions.explanation: Flexible sigmoidoscopy with insufflation sensitively evaluates rectosigmoid integrity after pelvic dissection.
